  • Methods
  • Results
  • Conclusions and Summary

From Geography to Development: Does Economic Structure Mediate the Latitude–Income Association?

Author

Zekun Song

Published

April 26, 2026

Introduction

The latitude–income gradient is one of the most durable patterns in development economics: countries near the equator are poor; countries far from it are rich. Gallup and Sachs (1999) made the case directly — tropical disease burdens, lower agricultural yields, and poor trade access hold equatorial nations back. But the pattern may be misread. Geography may not write income directly; it may instead shape structure first — the balance of farming, manufacturing, and services in a country’s economy — and structure shapes income.

This project uses 2019 cross-country data for 173 nations to test that claim. Does adding economic structure variables attenuate the latitude–income association, and by how much? Three sub-questions frame the analysis:

  1. Do geographic characteristics (latitude, landlocked status) predict economic structure (agriculture dependence, urbanisation, trade openness)?
  2. Does economic structure predict national income (GDP per capita)?
  3. Can machine learning classify high-income countries from geographic and structural features — and which variables carry the weight?

Economic structure appears to account for roughly half the raw geography–income gap. Geography persists beyond it, suggesting unmeasured factors — institutional, historical, or otherwise — remain relevant.

Methods

Data Sources and Acquisition

Data were obtained from two APIs. REST Countries (restcountries.com/v3.1/all) provided geographic metadata — latitude, landlocked status, and region — for all sovereign nations. World Bank Development Indicators (api.worldbank.org/v2) provided five 2019 economic indicators: GDP per capita (NY.GDP.PCAP.CD), trade as a share of GDP (NE.TRD.GNFS.ZS), urban population share (SP.URB.TOTL.IN.ZS), agriculture/forestry/fishing share of GDP (NV.AGR.TOTL.ZS), and total population (SP.POP.TOTL). Both APIs were queried programmatically and responses were cached to ensure reproducibility.

Data Cleaning and Wrangling

The cleaning pipeline ran in six steps, summarised in Table 1. REST Countries was filtered to valid three-letter ISO codes (\(n = 241\)). The World Bank response excluded aggregate and regional codes (“WLD”, “HIC”, etc.) via a curated exclusion list. An inner join on ISO3 produced 214 matched countries. Rows missing latitude and duplicate ISO3 entries — both zero — were removed. All variables were coerced to numeric and checked for plausibility (GDP > 0; urbanisation, agriculture share ∈ [0, 100]; trade share ≥ 0). A complete-case filter on all five core indicators yielded the final 173-country sample.

Missingness is concentrated in lower-capacity states: trade share drove most of the drop (\(n = 34\), largely small island economies), and agriculture share contributed a further \(n = 18\). The sample tilts toward countries with stronger statistical systems, which suggests the agriculture–income gradient in the results may be understated relative to the full global distribution.

GDP per capita is strongly right-skewed (skewness ≈ 2.1). Log-transforming it produces a near-symmetric distribution and stabilises variance for OLS — without the transform, Monaco and Luxembourg dominate the residuals. Population gets the same treatment for the same reason.

Feature Engineering

Seven features were engineered for the classification task. Distance from equator and its square encode the geographic gradient without forcing linearity — the linear term turns out dispensable; the squared term is not. Tropical (1 if |latitude| < 23.5°) adds a hard cut at the temperate divide. Log trade compresses the heavy right tail of trade share. Two interaction terms capture compounding effects: agriculture share × landlocked (structural disadvantage amplified by isolation) and trade share × urbanisation (openness amplified by density). Region encoded lets the models absorb continental-level patterns that the other features cannot fully capture.

Variable Selection

Feature selection used LASSO regression with 5-fold cross-validation predicting log GDP per capita. All 12 candidate features were standardised (\(\mu = 0\), \(\sigma = 1\)) before fitting. The penalty \(\alpha\) was chosen over a 50-point log-linear grid (\(\alpha \in [10^{-4},\, 10]\)) by held-out MSE; the optimal value was \(\alpha = 0.0043\). Eleven of twelve features survived. The one zeroed out — linear distance from equator — had its squared counterpart retained, consistent with a non-linear income gradient that steepens at higher latitudes.

Statistical Analysis

Analysis proceeds in three layers. Pearson correlations and Welch \(t\)-tests establish bivariate structure. Five nested OLS models (M1–M5) with HC3 heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors then track how the latitude coefficient moves as structure variables are added block by block — HC3 because cross-country income data is heteroskedastic by construction.

The classification task uses a stratified 80/20 split (\(n_\text{train} = 138\), \(n_\text{test} = 35\)) with 5-fold CV for hyperparameter tuning. Three models are compared: Logistic Regression as the linear baseline (regularisation strength \(C\) tuned by cross-validation), Random Forest (200 trees; depth and leaf-size via grid search), and XGBoost (200 estimators; depth, learning rate, and subsample ratio via grid search). The target is binary high-income status — GDP per capita above the World Bank threshold of $12,535. Performance is evaluated on accuracy, F1, and AUC-ROC.

K-Means (K = 2–8) on the three standardised structure variables provides an independent, unsupervised country typology. K is selected by silhouette score.

Results

Descriptive Statistics

The sample spans nearly two orders of magnitude: from $449 (Central African Republic) to $115,798 (Bermuda) in GDP per capita, or 6.1 to 11.7 on the log scale. Twenty-one per cent of countries (\(n = 37\)) are landlocked; 36% clear the high-income threshold. Agriculture share runs from near zero in service-dominated economies to 41% (Syria) — a range that will drive most of the predictive signal downstream.

Geography, Economic Structure, and Income

Distance from the equator correlates negatively with agriculture dependence (\(r = -0.39\), \(p < 0.001\)) and positively with urbanisation (\(r = 0.38\), \(p < 0.001\)). Both directions are expected: temperate economies moved out of farming first. The latitude–income association is strong (\(r = 0.55\), \(p < 0.001\)). Landlocked status, by contrast, does not independently predict trade share once other variables are held constant (Welch \(t\)-test: \(p = 0.52\)) — the structural constraint dominates the geographic one.

Figure 1 shows the central result directly. The latitude coefficient starts at 0.045 in M1 and falls monotonically as structure variables enter — almost entirely driven by M3 (agriculture share), which alone accounts for 42 percentage points of the 51% total attenuation. After M3, the slope flattens: urbanisation adds a small increment; trade adds almost nothing. The coefficient in M5 (0.022) is still highly significant (\(p < 0.001\)), meaning geography is not explained away — half of it is structural in origin, half is not.

Agriculture share is the strongest single predictor of income (\(r = -0.79\)), followed by urbanisation (\(r = 0.72\)). The two are not redundant — they capture orthogonal dimensions of the same transformation. Agrarian economies are rural and income-poor; urbanised service economies are dense and income-rich. The development path runs through both simultaneously.

Machine Learning: Classifying High-Income Countries

All three classifiers perform well. Random Forest leads on both AUC-ROC (0.958) and F1 (0.846); XGBoost follows (AUC = 0.937); Logistic Regression holds close behind (AUC = 0.927). The narrow gap between the linear and nonlinear models is informative: a logistic boundary capturing 93% of the AUC suggests the two income classes occupy largely distinct regions in this feature space.

Agriculture share ranks first across all three models; urbanisation is second in each case. The consistency across methods suggests the ranking reflects the data rather than any single algorithm’s inductive bias. Geographic features (landlocked, tropical, region) contribute modestly but appear in all three models, which is consistent with the OLS finding that geography retains predictive power even after conditioning on structure.

All three ROC curves hug the upper-left corner, confirming that the classifiers are not trading true-positive rate for false-positive rate at chance levels. The Random Forest curve lies slightly above the others across most threshold values, though the gap to XGBoost is small and would likely narrow on a larger test set. Logistic Regression’s AUC (0.927) approaching the nonlinear models (0.958, 0.937) is the more interpretable result: a linear decision boundary captures nearly as much discriminative information as ensemble methods, which implies the high-income class occupies a coherent, nearly linearly separable region in feature space.

K-Means Clustering

Both criteria agree on K = 3: the elbow plot shows diminishing inertia returns beyond K = 3, and the silhouette score peaks at K = 3 (score = 0.47).

Three clusters emerge from K-Means on the standardised structure variables. Agrarian economies have high agriculture dependence (mean 21%) and low urbanisation (38%), with mean GDP per capita around $3,000 — Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia dominate this group. Mixed/Transitional economies show sharply lower agriculture (4%) and substantially higher urbanisation (73%), with mean GDP around $22,000; the group spans emerging economies across Asia, Latin America, and Southern Europe. Urban Service economies have near-zero agriculture (0.5%), very high urbanisation (89%), and mean GDP per capita of $55,818 — concentrated in Western Europe and North America. Three variables cluster 173 countries into a recognisable development typology.

The alignment between cluster membership and income status is strong. All but one Agrarian-cluster country is non-high-income (55/56); six of seven Urban Service countries are high-income. Economic structure variables — without geographic coordinates or institutional data — appear sufficient to recover most of the income divide. The Mixed/Transitional cluster is structurally intermediate, and its income composition reflects that: the group spans both categories, consistent with economies that are mid-way through the structural transformation.

Interactive Visualizations

Three interactive figures are available at the interactive visualizations page: a world map coloured by cluster and sized by GDP per capita, an agriculture-vs-income scatter with hover tooltips, and an overlaid histogram comparing log GDP distributions across income groups.

Conclusions and Summary

The central question was whether economic structure mediates the latitude–income association. The results are consistent with partial mediation: the latitude coefficient attenuates by 51% once agriculture share and urbanisation enter the model, and K-Means clustering on structural variables alone recovers the income divide closely. But geography does not dissolve — the coefficient in M5 remains highly significant (\(p < 0.001\)). That persistent residual likely reflects unmeasured factors — institutions, colonial history, disease environment — that structural variables cannot fully absorb.

The machine learning results are consistent with this picture. All three classifiers achieve AUC above 0.92, and the narrow gap between Logistic Regression and Random Forest (0.927 vs 0.958) suggests the two income classes are largely separable in the feature space constructed here. The narrow model gap indicates the feature representation is doing most of the discriminative work; architecture choice contributes relatively little.

Limitations. The analysis is cross-sectional — 2019 is a single slice, and causal claims are not available. The 39-country attrition concentrated in low-capacity states compresses the lower tail: the agriculture–income gradient is likely stronger in the full global population than this sample shows. The geographic proxies are coarse — latitude captures distance from the equator, not tropical disease burden, colonial extraction, or maritime access, which are the actual mechanisms the literature invokes. The test set is small (\(n = 35\)); the AUC estimates carry real uncertainty.

What comes next. The question this design cannot answer is whether structural change causes income growth or income growth enables structural change — the classic Kuznets-curve problem. A panel from 1990–2020 would allow fixed-effects models to separate within-country dynamics from between-country levels. Adding institutional quality data would close the gap between the measured and unmeasured layers. And the non-linearity flagged by LASSO — why the squared latitude term survives while the linear term does not — deserves a dedicated investigation.
